title: Ward Delineation: NSA Can’t Allocate Land to Ijaw in Warri South LGA — Itsekiri Council
description: The Itsekiri National Youth Council (INYC) has vowed to resist any alleged attempt by National Security Adviser, Mallam Nuhu Ribadu, to allocate Itsekiri land to the Ijaw in Warri South Local Government Area of Delta State, insisting that the area remains the ancestral homeland of the Itsekiri people. In a statement signed by its President, […]
content:
The Itsekiri National Youth Council (INYC) has vowed to resist any alleged attempt by National Security Adviser, Mallam Nuhu Ribadu, to allocate Itsekiri land to the Ijaw in Warri South Local Government Area of Delta State, insisting that the area remains the ancestral homeland of the Itsekiri people.

In a statement signed by its President, Appearance Afejuku, the council declared that Itsekiri youths are prepared to defend their heritage and territorial integrity against what they described as attempts to alter the historical and legal status of communities within Warri South.

The council maintained that the Supreme Court had already settled issues relating to the ownership and identity of communities in Warri South, stressing that there are no Ijaw communities in the local government area.

According to the statement, the Itsekiri youths are not part of any ongoing negotiations involving government agencies, political leaders, or other groups concerning ward delineation and sharing arrangements in Warri South LGA.

“Whatever the political class and elders are negotiating with any group or government agency does not bind us as youths,” the statement said.

“We wish to remind the government that the youths are the future of the present, and we are ready to defend our collective heritage”.

The council urged the relevant authorities to ensure that all decisions regarding ward delineation and electoral boundaries in Warri South are guided by historical facts, judicial pronouncements.
summary: The dispute over land allocation in Warri South LGA of Delta State may have implications for the Lagos real estate market, particularly for developers with interests in the Lekki corridor, as it could lead to increased regulatory scrutiny and potential delays in project execution.

mixta_relevance.direct_impact: This development may not have a direct impact on our strategic priorities, but it could influence our approach to land acquisition and project execution in the Lekki corridor.
mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: The increased regulatory scrutiny and potential delays in project execution may lead to higher costs and reduced profitability for developers in the Lekki corridor.
mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: This development presents an opportunity for Mixta Africa to engage with stakeholders and demonstrate our commitment to responsible and sustainable development practices.
mixta_relevance.risk_flag: This development poses a risk to our execution timeline and costs, particularly if regulatory scrutiny leads to delays in project approval and land acquisition.

title: Insecurity: Forum Seeks Establishment Of Military Cantonment In Oyo, Offers Land
description: Insecurity: Forum Seeks Establishment Of Military Cantonment In Oyo, Offers Land  LEADERSHIP Newspapers
content: The Oyo Alafin Society (Oyo Forum) has sought the establishment of a military cantonment to enhance the Armed Forces’ presence and serve as a strategic response to banditry, kidnapping, and other criminal activities in the State. They disclosed this during a visit to the Permanent Secretary, Ministry of Defence, Mr Richard Pheelangwah, in Abuja. A statement by the Chief Information Officer of the Ministry, Queenet Iheoma-Hart, said the delegation from the Alafin of Oyo, under the umbrella of the Oyo Alafin Society (Oyo Forum), came to discuss partnership opportunities to address security challenges in Oyo State and its environs. She said the delegation conveyed the mandate of His Imperial Majesty, Iku Baba Yeye, Alayeluwa Oba Abimbola Akeem Owoade I, Alaafin of Oyo. The group said the Oyo community had resolved to make available a vast expanse of land for the construction of a military cantonment. “The proposed facility is expected to enhance the presence of the Armed Forces and serve as a strategic response to banditry, kidnapping, and other criminal activities,”. The Permanent Secretary, Mr Pheelangwah, described the initiative as noble. He thanked the delegation and assured them that the Ministry would give the proposal due consideration. He noted that a positive response would require the execution of a Memorandum of Understanding following due process. Speaking on behalf of the delegation, the Director-General/Chief Executive Officer of the National Board for Technology Incubation, Engr. Kolawole Raji commended the Minister of Defence and the Ministry for their commitment to safeguarding lives and property nationwide. RELATED NEWS Rivers: Monarch Threatens To Drag Ogoni Youths To Court LG Campaign Flag-off: Eribo Vows Quick Action On Infrastructure, Security In Egor Flooding: NEMA Begins Sensitisation In Imo Market Engr. Raji disclosed that community leaders and stakeholders of the Oyo Alaafin had earmarked land bordering the Old Oyo National Park, near Otefon Town in Atiba Local Government Area, for the establishment of a major military formation. He said the location is suitable for a modern cantonment capable of accommodating the Nigerian Army, operational units of the Nigerian Air Force, military training schools, healthcare facilities, residential quarters, commercial centres, and other strategic security installations. In addition to providing land for infrastructure, the Oyo Alafin community pledged to collaborate with the Armed Forces of Nigeria by providing all lawful and necessary community support to facilitate the successful execution of the project. His Imperial Majesty, who was unavoidably absent, sent a goodwill message via recorded video to the Minister and Ministry officials, expressing optimism for a favourable federal response. Similar goodwill messages were delivered via video by Senator Yunus Abiodun Akintunde, representing Oyo Central, and Hon. Akeem Adeyemi, representing Oyo Federal Constituency. The Ministry’s directo
summary: The proposed establishment of a military cantonment in Oyo State may lead to increased security presence and potentially boost property values in Lagos, particularly in the premium segment, as investors seek safer locations.

title: How land reform enriches govt, squeezes landlords, tenants in Enugu
description: How land reform enriches govt, squeezes landlords, tenants in Enugu  The Guardian Nigeria News
content: How land reform enriches govt, squeezes landlords, tenants in Enugu By : Lawrence Njoku Date: 17 June 2026 4:00am WAT Share : Follow Us on Google Discover Enugu State Governor, Peter Mbah Enugu State Governor, Peter Mbah The move by the Enugu State government to raise revenues and revolutionise land administration is becoming burdensome on residents, landlords and investors, while the state government smiles to the bank, LAWRENCE NJOKU reports. Ezenwa Ogubuike, a retired civil servant, recently returned to his residence at Akwuke in Enugu South Local Council, Enugu State, to find a red mark on his gate with the bold inscription, “Not Paid, By ENGIS.” At first, Ogubuike did not understand the message until inquiries revealed that it was placed by officials of the state government. Having not paid any levy to the government since erecting the building, and given that the area has not enjoyed any government presence, he wondered why anyone could make such a demand. A few days later, Ogubuike visited the Enugu State Geographic Information System (ENGIS) office and was informed that the government had begun revalidating property in the area. The revalidation required property owners to present their documents after paying a non-refundable processing fee of N100,000 for privately acquired land. At the conclusion of the process, the property is authenticated and updated to conform to government land administration standards. Among other things, ENGIS is tasked with transforming land administration and spatial data management, revolutionising how individuals, investors, and the government interact with land and property information – ensuring accuracy, security, and transparency across all real estate transactions. Ogubuike said he was also expected to cough up “over N5m” to undertake the recertification process. To meet the recertification demand, however, he increased the rent for the four flats occupied by tenants by over 100 per cent. This automatically increased the rent of a building he set up six years ago from N400,000 to N800,000 per annum, even though there is no visible public infrastructure to attract people to the area. In the same area, owners of undeveloped plots of land were required to undergo similar processes to retain their property, as they were expected to present their documents alongside non-refundable fees to ENGIS and to pay the infrastructure levy to enable the government to fix the area. This is the situation that landlords in Olympic Layout, One Day Area, Merry Land Extension, Ugwuabor, Jamboree, and several other developing areas of the state face. While some were asked to recertify their property, others underwent regularisation and paid the infrastructure levy to the state government. While the state government continues to demand charges it said were part of state law, the burden is passed on to tenants by landlords through house/shop rents, which now threaten to erode the incomes of average earners in the state. Writing
summary: The Enugu State government's land reform may have implications for the Lagos real estate market, particularly in terms of regulatory environment and potential increases in costs for developers. This could lead to a shift in focus towards more investment-friendly states. However, the direct impact on Lagos is likely to be minimal, with the state's own regulatory framework and market dynamics taking precedence.

title: How Alfred Ijimakinwa is redefining property marketing
description: How Alfred Ijimakinwa is redefining property marketing  Vanguard News
content: By Jimoh Babatunde Real estate strategist Alfred Ijimakinwa says Nigeria’s property market suffers from slow sales, weak trust, and limited access — not land scarcity. To fix it, he launched Blue Earth Properties with a disruptive promise: sell your property in 60 days or less. Ijimakinwa, a business strategist with 15+ years’ experience, built Blue Earth as a marketing and brokerage firm that removes the sales burden from developers and private owners. The company uses “blitz marketing,” targeted social media campaigns, and a database of pre-qualified buyers to turn property sales from waiting games into measurable transactions. “Guesswork is expensive in real estate,” Ijimakinwa said. Under his leadership, Blue Earth now runs on NIESV-standard valuations and real market data. Comparable properties set prices, not emotion. Marketing targets decision-makers, not just views. Buyer screening filters out time-wasters. Documentation and post-sale support keep clients engaged after the deal closes. His systems approach comes from BusinessIQ Africa, the venture he founded to help over 1,000 entrepreneurs launch businesses across Africa. That background shaped Blue Earth’s culture: operate like a business partner, not just an agent, with clear structure and measurable KPIs. The bigger goal is inclusion. Ijimakinwa champions the idea that “everyone deserves a piece of real estate” through installment payment plans and fractional ownership. The models allow Nigerians with regular income to secure land over time and let multiple investors co-own income-generating properties. He said it directly challenges the notion that real estate is only for the elite. Today Blue Earth Properties offers valuation, fast-sales marketing, property management, and brokerage for houses, land, and apartments. The firm operates in Lagos and Enugu and plans expansion to Ibadan, Abuja, Ghana, and the UK to serve both local owners and diaspora investors. Recognition has followed the impact. Ijimakinwa recently received the NYAGGI Golden Role Model Award for youth empowerment and innovation in real estate, and an honorary doctorate fellowship in real estate development and project management from the Chartered Institute of Public Resources Management and Politics, Ghana. With Nigeria’s housing deficit widening and prime land values rising, Ijimakinwa argues that professional marketing and verified documentation are the missing links. “The quicker owners can sell, the faster developers can build more. That cycle is how we close the housing gap,” he said. Through Blue Earth Properties, he is turning property into opportunity — one verified deal at a time. Comments expressed here do not reflect the opinions of Vanguard newspapers or any employee thereof.
summary: Alfred Ijimakinwa's Blue Earth Properties is disrupting the Lagos property market with its blitz marketing approach, targeting decision-makers and using real market data to set prices, which may increase competition for Mixta Africa and impact our strategy to shift new-sales mix toward higher-margin segments.

mixta_relevance.direct_impact: This may increase competition for Mixta Africa's premium Lakowe Lakes products and LNT commercial plots, potentially affecting our strategy to shift new-sales mix toward higher-margin segments.
mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: The disruption in the market may lead to increased pricing transparency, which could benefit Mixta Africa's efforts to maintain DFI credibility and attract institutional investors.

title: Short-Term Rentals Gain Momentum Amid Nigeria’s Housing Supply Challenges
description: Short-Term Rentals Gain Momentum Amid Nigeria’s Housing Supply Challenges  nigeriahousingmarket.com
content: Short-Let Apartments Reshape Nigeria’s Rental Market Amid Rising Housing Demand housingnational news Jun 16 Written By Ayomide Fiyinfunoluwa Rising Popularity of Short Lets Creates New Opportunities for Property Investors Short-let apartments are increasingly reshaping Nigeria’s rental market, creating new opportunities for property investors while altering traditional housing patterns across major urban centres. Industry stakeholders say rising demand for flexible accommodation, tourism-related stays, business travel and remote work arrangements has accelerated the growth of the short-let segment, particularly in cities such as Lagos and Abuja. / You Might Also Like / The 2026 Dangote Refinery Blueprint: Location, IPO, and Global Energy Impact The Lagos-Calabar Coastal Highway: Transforming Nigeria’s Infrastructure Landscape Nigeria’s Housing Deficit: How Big Is the Problem in 2026? Mistakes First-Time Property Investors Make in Nigeria The trend reflects broader changes in Nigeria’s housing market, where tenants and travellers are seeking greater flexibility, while property owners pursue higher returns than those typically generated by conventional long-term rental arrangements. As demand continues to grow, analysts believe short-let apartments are becoming an increasingly influential component of the country’s real estate sector. Rising Demand Fuels Growth of Short-Let Accommodation The growing popularity of short-let apartments is being driven by changing consumer preferences and evolving work patterns. According to industry reports, the expansion of hybrid and remote work models has encouraged more professionals to seek flexible living arrangements that offer convenience and affordability. At the same time, increasing domestic and international travel has supported demand for furnished short-term accommodation in key commercial and tourism destinations. Property owners have responded by converting residential units into short-let apartments, attracted by the potential for higher earnings compared with traditional annual tenancy agreements. Market participants say short-term rentals often generate stronger cash flow, particularly in high-demand locations. Impact on the Traditional Rental Market The rapid expansion of short-let accommodation is generating debate within Nigeria’s housing sector. Some housing experts argue that the conversion of residential properties into short-term rentals may reduce the supply of homes available for long-term tenants, potentially contributing to rising rents in already constrained housing markets. Lagos, in particular, continues to experience strong demand for housing amid a significant supply deficit and growing urban population. As more landlords pursue short-let opportunities, industry observers warn that the shift could place additional pressure on affordability, especially in prime residential districts where rental demand remains strong. Investor Interest Continues to Increase Despite concerns about aff
summary: The rising popularity of short-let apartments in Nigeria's major urban centers, particularly Lagos, presents an opportunity for property investors and may alter traditional housing patterns, potentially impacting Mixta Africa's strategic priorities and active projects. This trend reflects broader changes in Nigeria's housing market, driven by rising demand for flexible accommodation and remote work arrangements. As a result, Mixta Africa may need to adapt its sales strategies to capitalize on this emerging trend.

title: Oxgital launches ‘Sack Your Landlord’ campaign, unveils Saka as Citi Buildings’ Brand Ambassador
description: Oxgital launches ‘Sack Your Landlord’ campaign, unveils Saka as Citi Buildings’ Brand Ambassador  Vanguard News
content: Lagos-based leading marketing and advertising agency Oxgital has announced the signing of popular Nigerian veteran actor, comedian and entertainer Dr Hafiz Oyetoro (aka) Saka, popularly known for his viral phrase “I don port o”, as the brand ambassador for Citi Hope Homes, the flagship residential estate project of Citi Buildings Real Estate and Property Development Company. The announcement also signals the official launch of the Sack Your Landlord campaign, a movement that goes straight at one of Nigeria’s most interesting conversations: “that homeownership is something only the rich can afford.” Citi Hope Homes is a master-planned residential estate located in Obafemi Owode, Ogun State, offering Nigerians a flexible and legally secured path to homeownership. A spokesperson for Citi Buildings described the collaboration as a natural fit for the brand’s mission. “Saka is not just a celebrity to us. He represents the everyday Nigerian, the person who works hard, who understands struggle, and who still believes that better is possible. That is exactly who Citi Hope Homes was built for. We did not want a face that looked like success was easy. We wanted a face that looks like success is earned. Saka is that person. This campaign is going to change the conversation around homeownership in this country.” For Oxgital, the agency behind the campaign strategy and execution, the Sack Your Landlord campaign represents something bigger than a property-ownership launch. “We build campaigns that mean something,” said Akeem Osungbade, Chief Executive Officer at Oxgital. “When we sat down with Citi Buildings and looked at what they were actually offering- ₦2,400 a day, full legal title, a real estate developer with nearly a decade of track record, we knew this was a movement waiting to happen” “Saka was the man. We needed someone who speaks to the everyday person the way he does. This campaign is going to be loud; it is going to be everywhere, and most importantly, it is going to put real Nigerians in real homes.” The Citi Hope Homes estate is situated in Obafemi Owode, Ogun State, offering residents proximity to the Abeokuta State House, the Federal Medical Centre, and the Abeokuta Railway Station, with easy access from Lagos via the major expressway. The Sack Your Landlord campaign is set to roll out across digital, outdoor, and broadcast platforms with OXGITAL AT THE HEART OF IT Comments expressed here do not reflect the opinions of Vanguard newspapers or any employee thereof.
summary: The launch of Oxgital's 'Sack Your Landlord' campaign and Citi Hope Homes' partnership with Saka may increase demand for affordable housing in Lagos, potentially benefiting Mixta Africa's premium Lakowe Lakes products and LNT commercial plots.

title: Resida Targets Nigeria’s Rental Market With New Housing App
description: Resida Targets Nigeria’s Rental Market With New Housing App  Business Post Nigeria
content: By Adedapo Adesanya Resida, a property technology startup focused on modernising the rental experience, has officially launched its all-in-one housing platform designed to simplify how Nigerians find homes, pay rent and manage property-related services. The platform enters the market at a time when renters across major Nigerian cities continue to face challenges such as fake property listings, fragmented rental processes, poor record keeping and difficulty accessing reliable maintenance services. Resida brings these services together within a single ecosystem, allowing users to search verified and inspected properties, apply for shortlet or long-term rentals, make secure rent payments and access trusted artisans for repairs and maintenance. According to the company, the platform was built to address long-standing inefficiencies in Nigeria’s rental market by providing a more transparent and structured experience for tenants, landlords and property managers. “Renting in Lagos has never had the infrastructure it deserves. For too long, the rental experience has been stressful, fragmented and built on trust gaps,” said Mr Ilemobayo Kings Ayejuni, Founder of Resida. “We built Resida because tenants, agents and landlords deserve a process that is transparent, secure and simple, from the first property search to rent payment and ongoing maintenance,” he said. Beyond helping renters find homes, the platform offers landlords and property managers tools to streamline operations, including tenant verification, automated rent collection, financial reporting and maintenance request tracking. The company says it is currently active in more than 15 locations across Lagos, with a growing inventory of verified properties available to renters across the city. The launch reflects growing innovation within Nigeria’s property technology sector, where startups are increasingly using digital solutions to address challenges in housing access and rental management. Available on Android, iOS and the web, the Resida app enables users to discover verified properties, apply for rentals, manage rent payments, keep track of tenancy records and connect with trusted artisans for maintenance needs, all from a single platform designed to make renting simpler and more reliable. This development may also lead to increased competition in the rental market, pressuring prices and occupancy rates. As a result, Mixta Africa may need to adapt its pricing and marketing strategies to remain competitive.

title: Deregistration Of Parties Confirms Plot To Undermine Democracy, Says Olawepo-Hashim
description: Deregistration Of Parties Confirms Plot To Undermine Democracy, Says Olawepo-Hashim  LEADERSHIP Newspapers
content: The Co-Chairman of the Movement for Democratic Renewal (MDR) and presidential candidate of the Accord Party, Dr. Gbenga Olawepo-Hashim, has described the Federal High Court’s order directing the deregistration of five political parties, including the African Democratic Congress (ADC) and Accord Party, as confirmation of what he called a deliberate plot to weaken opposition politics and undermine Nigeria’s democracy ahead of the 2027 general elections. In a statement issued by his campaign office on Tuesday, Olawepo-Hashim said the court ruling validated concerns he had repeatedly raised since 2024 regarding an alleged coordinated strategy aimed at making President Bola Ahmed Tinubu the only viable presidential contender in the 2027 election. According to him, recent developments in the country’s political environment point to a systematic and multi-layered effort to weaken opposition parties and restrict political competition. He recalled repeatedly warning that opposition governors were being pressured to defect to the ruling All Progressives Congress (APC), alleging that those who resisted were subjected to intimidation and political blackmail. “Those who refused are hunted and blackmailed,” he stated. Olawepo-Hashim argued that after securing the defection of several opposition governors, attention shifted to weakening opposition political parties at the national level. The Accord Party presidential candidate further alleged that individuals sympathetic to the ruling party had been deployed to destabilise major opposition platforms through internal disputes and leadership crises. According to him, the factional conflicts that have plagued some opposition parties over the past two years were not accidental but formed part of a broader strategy to weaken alternative political platforms before the next general election. Hashim also criticised the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC), accusing the electoral body of implementing an election timetable that he claimed was inconsistent with provisions of the Electoral Act 2026. He maintained that despite a court judgment challenging aspects of the timetable, INEC had failed to make the necessary adjustments, thereby creating obstacles for opposition parties. Referring specifically to the Federal High Court’s order directing the deregistration of the ADC, Accord Party and three other political parties, Hashim described the ruling as the most significant step yet in what he alleged was a wider plan to shrink Nigeria’s democratic space. “This is not only to destabilise the parties, but to ensure that the parties are not able to survive the illegal INEC processes to ensure only Tinubu can be a viable candidate,” he said. “With the latest development from the Federal High Court ordering deregistration of opposition political parties, it is now clear beyond any reasonable doubt that President Tinubu is determined to kill Nigeria’s democracy.” Olawepo-Hashim noted that Nigeria’s founding fathers
summary: The deregistration of political parties may lead to increased political instability, potentially affecting investor confidence and demand in the Lagos real estate market, particularly in the premium and commercial segments. This could pressurize new launches and occupancy rates in developments like Lakowe Lakes and Lagos New Town. The impact on the market may be felt in the medium to long term.

mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: Potential decrease in demand and investor confidence, affecting pricing and sales velocity
mixta_relevance.risk_flag: Increased political instability may threaten execution of strategic priorities, particularly those reliant on investor confidence and demand

title: Drekford Wins Real Estate Award as Firm Expands Footprint in Nigeria’s Property Market
description: Drekford Wins Real Estate Award as Firm Expands Footprint in Nigeria’s Property Market  nigeriahousingmarket.com
content: Drekford Wins Real Estate Award as Firm Expands Footprint in Nigeria’s Property Market housingNational news Jun 16 Written By Ayomide Fiyinfunoluwa Drekford Wins Prestigious Real Estate Award for Sector Contributions Drekford has reinforced its standing in Nigeria’s real estate sector after receiving a prestigious industry award that recognises its contributions to property development, innovation and market growth. The recognition reflects the company’s expanding presence within the country's competitive real estate landscape and highlights its role in delivering residential and investment-focused property solutions. / You Might Also Like / The 2026 Dangote Refinery Blueprint: Location, IPO, and Global Energy Impact The Lagos-Calabar Coastal Highway: Transforming Nigeria’s Infrastructure Landscape Nigeria’s Housing Deficit: How Big Is the Problem in 2026? Mistakes First-Time Property Investors Make in Nigeria The award comes at a time when Nigeria’s property sector continues to evolve amid rising demand for housing, urban expansion and growing investor interest in real estate assets. Industry observers say such recognitions often serve as indicators of corporate performance, market credibility and long-term commitment to sector development. Recognition for Industry Excellence According to the organisers, the award acknowledges Drekford’s achievements in delivering quality real estate projects and maintaining high professional standards within the industry. The company has built a reputation around property development, investment opportunities and customer-focused housing solutions, positioning itself among emerging players in Nigeria’s growing real estate market. Industry awards are increasingly viewed as important benchmarks that recognise innovation, operational excellence and contributions to economic development within the property sector. Growing Presence in Nigeria’s Property Market The recognition highlights Drekford’s efforts to strengthen its footprint in a market characterised by increasing demand for residential housing and real estate investment products. Nigeria’s housing sector continues to face a significant supply gap, creating opportunities for developers that can deliver quality projects while addressing affordability and infrastructure challenges. As urbanisation accelerates across major cities, developers are increasingly focusing on housing delivery, land development and investment-driven projects to meet changing market needs. Drekford’s growing visibility within the sector reflects broader trends of expansion and competition among private developers seeking to capture opportunities within the real estate market. Real Estate Remains Key Investment Asset The award also underscores the importance of real estate as a long-term investment vehicle in Nigeria. Despite economic challenges, property remains one of the country's most resilient asset classes, attracting both institutional and individual investors seeking capital app
summary: Drekford's real estate award reinforces the growing competition in Lagos' property market, potentially pressuring Mixta Africa to adapt its sales mix and pricing strategies to remain competitive.

mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: Mixta Africa can capitalize on its existing relationships with institutional investors and bulk buyers to pursue larger transactions and maintain its market share.
mixta_relevance.risk_flag: The increased competition may threaten Mixta Africa's execution of its strategic priorities, particularly in terms of recovering concentrated receivables and maintaining DFI credibility.

title: How to buy land in Nigeria without falling victim to fraud
description: How to buy land in Nigeria without falling victim to fraud  TheCable
content: Stay connected via Google News Many Nigerians living abroad consider buying land back home to be more than an investment. It is often the realisation of a long-held dream. The Nigerian diaspora continues to invest in land, whether to build a family home, establish a business, prepare for retirement or simply maintain a connection with their roots. Unfortunately, it is also one of the most vulnerable areas for fraud, where many innocent buyers lose a lot of money every year. The allure of owning property in Nigeria is understandable. In many parts of the country, particularly in rapidly growing urban and suburban areas, land values continue to climb. However, the very opportunities that attract genuine investors also appeal to fraudsters who take advantage of the distance, trust and limited access to information that often characterise diaspora transactions. A constant challenge for Nigerians abroad is the inability to physically inspect properties before making payments. Many, therefore, depend heavily on relatives, friends, estate agents or developers who say they have found suitable opportunities. These people may be acting in good faith, but their involvement does not relieve the obligation to properly verify. Indeed, many fraudulent transactions have happened precisely because buyers have blindly trusted third parties, without carrying out the necessary due diligence. One common example is when a potential buyer is shown photos and videos of a parcel of land through social media or messaging apps. Often, the seller creates a sense of urgency about the offer, claiming there are several interested buyers for the property and that payment must be made immediately to secure the purchase. In the race to buy land and the fear of missing out, some buyers transfer huge amounts of money without even verifying the ownership and authenticity of the documents shown to them. They later find that the seller had no legal right to sell the land, the property had been sold to someone else, or the land itself is subject to competing claims and disputes. Advertisement The fraud of land in Nigeria has many sides. Some people pretend to be owners when they are actually caretakers, family members, tenants or agents without the authority to transfer title. At other times, forged title documents such as Certificates of Occupancy, survey plans and deeds of assignment are produced to look real. There have also been many cases where one piece of land has been sold to more than one purchaser, each receiving a deed purporting to establish ownership. These risks underline the importance of thorough investigations before spending money on any property transaction. Prospective buyers should always try to confirm ownership and the legal status of the land, no matter how convincing a seller is or how tempting the offer. A title search will tell you if the seller owns the property, if there are any encumbrances on the property, if the land is subject to acquisition by the gove
summary: The article highlights the risks of fraud in land transactions in Nigeria, particularly for diaspora buyers, which may impact the Lagos real estate market by reducing demand and increasing scrutiny of property purchases. This could affect Mixta Africa's efforts to grow the diaspora buyer channel through the 'Own It 4 Sure' framework. The article's focus on fraud prevention may also lead to increased awareness and demand for secure, trustworthy property transactions.

mixta_relevance.direct_impact: The article's emphasis on fraud prevention may impact Mixta Africa's diaspora sales efforts, requiring increased transparency and security measures to attract and retain buyers.
mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: The article may lead to increased regulatory scrutiny of property transactions, potentially affecting Mixta Africa's operations and compliance costs.
mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: Mixta Africa can capitalize on the article's focus on secure property transactions by highlighting its own secure and trustworthy processes, potentially attracting more diaspora buyers.
mixta_relevance.risk_flag: The article's discussion of fraud risks may deter some potential buyers, posing a risk to Mixta Africa's sales targets and revenue growth.
